We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!
